name: Saudi Foul with Tahini

ingredients.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Warm the beans with a splash of their liquid over medium-low heat, 5-6 minutes.
Mash lightly, leaving some texture, and stir in the tahini, lime juice, garlic, oil, and paprika.
Serve with warm bread - foul is a common breakfast across the Arabian Peninsula, and Saudi versions are often finished generously with tahini.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Warm the beans with a splash of their liquid over medium-low heat, 5-6 minutes.
Mash lightly, leaving some texture, and stir in the tahini, lime juice, garlic, oil, and paprika.
Serve with warm bread - foul is a common breakfast across the Arabian Peninsula, and Saudi versions are often finished generously with tahini.
Real fava beans are used here now rather than a stand-in - they're a genuinely different bean with their own texture and flavor.

name: Saudi Arabic Coffee with Dates
steps: Serve the dates alongside a small cup of Arabic coffee (qahwa), traditionally lightly spiced with cardamom.
steps_detailed: Arrange the dates on a small plate, with pistachios if using.

Serve alongside a small cup of qahwa, Saudi Arabic coffee, traditionally brewed pale and lightly spiced with cardamom - dates and coffee together are a deeply rooted symbol of hospitality across Saudi Arabia.

name: Saudi Balaleet
steps: Toss the cooked noodles with the sugar and turmeric.
Spread into a thin layer and pour the beaten egg over the top.
Cook over low heat, covered, 4-5 minutes until the egg is set.
steps_detailed: Toss the cooked noodles with the sugar, turmeric, and rosewater.
Melt the butter in a skillet, spread the sweetened noodles in an even layer, and pour the beaten egg over the top.
Cover and cook over low heat 5-7 minutes until the egg is fully set.
Balaleet, a sweet-and-savory vermicelli dish, is a distinctive breakfast found across Saudi Arabia and the Gulf.

name: Saudi Kabsa
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the rice, tomatoes, turmeric, and 1.5 cups water, cover, and simmer 20-22 minutes.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken in the oil, 8-10 minutes, then set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic in the same pot, 6-8 minutes, until deeply golden.
Add the tomatoes, paprika, cardamom, cinnamon, and pepper, and cook 3-4 minutes until fragrant.
Return the chicken with 1.75 cups water, the pierced dried limes, and saffron, and simmer 20-25 minutes.
Lift out the chicken, stir the rice into the liquid, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes until tender.
Fluff the rice and top with the chicken.
Kabsa's own kabsa spice blend varies by household, but paprika, cardamom, cinnamon, and dried lime together are the throughline that makes it recognizably kabsa rather than a generic pilaf.

name: Saudi Jareesh
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the bulgur and broth, and simmer, covered, 20-25 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick and porridge-like.
Serve hot.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, and cinnamon over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the bulgur (a reasonable stand-in for jareesh, cracked wheat) and chicken broth.
Cover and simmer 22-30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick, tender, and porridge-like.
Jareesh is a traditional, hearty Saudi dish, especially popular in the Eastern Province.

name: Saudi Mandi
steps: Roast the chicken at 400F for 30-35 minutes.
Simmer the rice with the onion and cardamom in 1.5 cups water, covered, 20-22 minutes, and serve together.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken with half the cardamom and the cinnamon, and roast at 425F (220C), skin-side up on a rack over a pan, 30-35 minutes, until deeply browned - the point is for the fat and juices to drip down rather than staying in contact with the meat.
Meanwhile, soften the onion in the oil in a separate pot, 5-6 minutes, then add the remaining cardamom, cloves, saffron, and 1.75 cups water and bring to a simmer.

Position the pot of simmering spiced water directly under the roasting rack for the last 20-25 minutes of roasting, so the dripping chicken fat and juices fall straight into the liquid, then stir in the rice, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes.
Fluff the rice and serve the chicken on top or alongside.

Mandi's whole identity is this drip technique - meat suspended above, rice cooked below in what falls from it - traditionally done in an underground pit oven called a tandoor here approximated with a roasting rack over a pot; it's genuinely a different dish from kabsa, not just a spelling variant, because the rice never touches the meat directly while cooking.

name: Saudi Shorba
steps: Brown the lamb.
Add the bulgur, tomatoes, and broth, and simmer 20-25 minutes until the grain is tender.
Serve hot, traditionally to break the fast during Ramadan.
steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with the onion and turmeric, 6-8 minutes.
Add the bulgur, diced tomatoes, and chicken broth, and simmer 22-28 minutes until the grain is tender and the soup has thickened.
Finish with the lime juice - shorba is a warming, restorative soup, especially cherished for breaking the fast during Ramadan.

name: Saudi Lamb Kabsa
steps: Brown the lamb with the onion.
Add the rice, tomatoes, turmeric, and 1.5 cups water, cover, and simmer 20-22 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: formed into a few large chunks

steps_detailed: Brown the lamb in the oil, 9-11 minutes, then set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic, 6-8 minutes, until deeply golden.
Add the tomatoes, paprika, cardamom, cinnamon, and nutmeg, and cook 3-4 minutes.
Return the lamb with 2 cups water, the pierced dried limes, and saffron, cover, and simmer 40-45 minutes until very tender.
Lift out the lamb, stir the rice into the liquid, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes.
Fluff the rice and top with the lamb.

name: Saudi Grilled Chicken (Farrouj Mashwi)
steps: Marinate the chicken with the turmeric, garlic, and lime juice for at least 20 minutes.
Grill or roast at 425F for 30-35 minutes until well browned and cooked through.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the chicken with the turmeric, garlic, lime juice, oil, and cinnamon for at least 30 minutes, or overnight.
Grill over medium-high heat, or roast at 425F (220C), turning occasionally, 32-38 minutes until deeply browned and cooked through.
Serve with rice - grilled, spice-marinated chicken is a Saudi staple for family meals and gatherings.

name: Saudi Saleeg
steps: Simmer the chicken in water until cooked, then shred.
Cook the rice in the milk with the cardamom until thick and creamy, 30 minutes, and stir the chicken through.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken with the garlic in 1.5 cups water, 20-22 minutes, until cooked through.
Lift out the chicken and shred; reserve the broth.

Add the rice and milk to the broth, along with the cardamom and pepper, and simmer over low heat, stirring often, 30-35 minutes, well past the point rice normally softens, until it breaks down into a thick, smooth, savory porridge.
Stir in the butter, top with the shredded chicken.

Saleeg, rice cooked down in milk and broth until creamy rather than kept fluffy and separate, is a completely different rice texture from kabsa or mandi - the milk and the deliberate overcooking are what define it, not a mistake in either direction.

name: Saudi Margoog
steps: Brown the lamb.
Make a simple dough from the flour and water, tear into flat pieces, and add to the pot with the sweet potato and tomatoes.
Simmer 25-30 minutes until everything is tender.
steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with the onion and turmeric, 8-10 minutes.
Make a simple, firm dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and tear or roll into flat, irregular pieces.
Add the dough pieces, sweet potato, and diced tomatoes to the pot along with 2 cups water.
Simmer 25-32 minutes until the dough pieces and vegetables are tender and the stew has thickened.
Margoog is a beloved traditional Saudi stew, its torn dough pieces giving it a distinctive, rustic texture.

name: Saudi Sambousek
steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Make a simple dough from the flour and water, roll thin, cut into rounds, fill, and fold into half-moons.
Fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion, turmeric, and cinnamon.
Make a simple, firm dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and roll out thin.
Cut into rounds, fill with the beef mixture, and fold into half-moons, crimping the edges well.
Fry until deeply golden and crisp on all sides, 4-5 minutes - sambousek is a beloved Saudi snack, especially popular during Ramadan.

name: Saudi Hummus

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, lime juice, and garlic with a splash of water until smooth.
Serve with warm bread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, lime juice, garlic, and oil with a splash of water until very smooth.
Spread onto a plate, drizzle with more oil, and serve with warm bread - hummus is common throughout the Arabian Peninsula.

name: Saudi Dates Stuffed with Nuts
steps: Split each date and press a walnut half into the center.
Serve as a simple, sweet snack.
steps_detailed: Split each date lengthwise and press a walnut half into the center, or fill with chopped pistachios.
Arrange on a plate and serve - stuffed dates are a common, elegant Saudi snack, especially offered to guests.

name: Saudi Spiced Mixed Nuts

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt
Nuts, pistachio nuts, dry roasted, with salt added

steps: Toss the nuts with the turmeric.
Warm briefly in a dry skillet, 2-3 minutes, and serve.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt
Nuts, pistachio nuts, dry roasted, with salt added

steps_detailed: Toss the nuts with the oil and turmeric.
Warm in a dry skillet over low heat, 2-4 minutes, tossing frequently, until fragrant.
Spiced nuts are a common accompaniment to Arabic coffee across Saudi Arabia, offered warmly to guests.

name: Saudi Muhalabia
steps: Whisk the milk, sugar, and cornstarch together.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, 5-6 minutes until thickened.
Stir in the rosewater and chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Whisk the milk, sugar, and cornstarch together until no lumps remain.
Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, 6-8 minutes until thick and pudding-like.
Stir in the rosewater off the heat, then pour into serving cups.
Chill at least 2 hours and top with chopped pistachios before serving - muhalabia is a beloved milk pudding across Saudi Arabia and the Gulf.

name: Saudi Date Cake (Ma'amoul-Style)
steps: Mix the flour, butter, and sugar into a soft, crumbly dough.
Fold in the mashed dates.
Press into a small pan and bake at 350F for 20-25 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, butter, sugar, and cinnamon into a soft, crumbly dough.
Fold in the mashed dates, distributing them evenly.
Press into a small buttered pan and bake at 350F (175C) for 22-28 minutes until golden.
Cool slightly before slicing - a simple, date-forward cake reflecting Saudi Arabia's abundance of dates.

name: Saudi Luqaimat
steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, and enough water to form a thick, smooth batter. Rest 20 minutes.
Drop spoonfuls into the hot oil and fry until puffed and golden, about 3-4 minutes.
Drizzle with the honey while still warm.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, sugar, turmeric if using (for a golden color), and enough warm water to form a thick, smooth, pourable batter. Cover and rest 25-30 minutes.
Drop spoonfuls into the hot oil and fry, turning occasionally, 3-5 minutes until puffed, deeply golden, and cooked through.
Drain briefly and drizzle generously with the honey while still warm - luqaimat, sweet fried dumplings, are a beloved treat across Saudi Arabia and the Gulf, especially during Ramadan.

name: Saudi Dagoos (Tomato Sauce)
steps: Simmer the tomatoes with the garlic and turmeric for 10-12 minutes until thickened.
Serve alongside rice or grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Saute the garlic and jalapeno in the oil briefly, then add the tomatoes and turmeric.
Simmer 12-15 minutes until thickened into a rich, savory sauce.
Dagoos is a simple tomato-based sauce found across Saudi Arabia, often served alongside rice or grilled meat.

name: Saudi Tahini Date Dip

ingredients.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Blend the tahini, mashed dates, and milk until smooth.
Serve as a sweet dip for bread or fruit.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Blend the tahini, mashed dates, milk, and cinnamon together until smooth and creamy.
Serve as a sweet dip for bread, fruit, or pancakes - a simple treat drawing on two of Saudi Arabia's most iconic ingredients.
